text.title: Architecture Review, LLM Patterns, and CI Integration
text.summary: Architecture review type selection (roadmap vs design), effective LLM usage patterns per workflow phase, decision tree for workflow selection, CI/CD quality gates, and key failure modes when agents drive architecture workflows.

list.item: Roadmap Review: answers "should we build this?" — validates business drivers, cost/benefit, and strategic fit. Timing: early planning, before any design work. Audience: stakeholders + architect.

Design Review: answers "is this design sound?" — validates requirements coverage, NFR targets, trade-offs, and implementation risk. Timing: before implementation begins. Audience: tech leads + implementers.
Risk-Storming: structured brainstorm of failure points. Each participant independently identifies risks, then consolidates. Focus: SPOFs, cascading failures, data-loss scenarios.
Security Review: threat-modeling pass (STRIDE or attack trees). Separate from design review to ensure the security lens is not diluted.
Post-Incident Review: architectural lessons from production incidents. Feed findings back into ADRs and utility trees.

rule.statement: Never combine Roadmap Review and Design Review in the same session. They serve different stakeholder audiences and different questions.
rule.rationale: Mixed sessions produce shallow coverage of both: stakeholders disengage during design details; implementers tune out during strategic framing.

list.item: New system from scratch → System Design Workflow. If complex with multiple teams → include ATAM step. If simple, single team → lightweight design only.
Evaluating an existing system → Architecture Review Workflow. If quality-attribute focus → add ATAM. If cost/benefit focus → add CBAM.
Making a significant technology decision → Technology Evaluation Workflow + ADR.
Documenting any non-obvious architectural decision → ADR Workflow (standalone).
Legacy system modernization → Migration Planning Workflow (Strangler Fig).
Sprint feature with architecture impact → Design Document Review Workflow.

rule.statement: Always run the Validate phase as a separate adversarial agent call, never as a closing paragraph appended to the Design call. Different prompt, different system context, explicit failure-finding instructions.
rule.rationale: The same persona that designed the system cannot adversarially attack it; the critic role requires a context switch enforced by prompt separation.

list.item: Requirements analysis: LLM extracts and categorizes stakeholder input into functional/non-functional lists. Human validates numeric NFR targets before proceeding.
Scale estimation: LLM calculates capacity needs from business metrics (DAU, request_per_user, data_per_event). Verify formulas, not just numbers.
Design generation: LLM produces three architecture options with explicit NFR scores. Reject single-option output.
ADR writing: LLM drafts from meeting notes or decision log. Human ensures the "context" sentence is a problem statement, not a solution description.
Technology research: LLM summarizes vendor comparisons. Require citations; mark uncited claims as unverified.
Risk identification: LLM brainstorms failure modes and edge cases. Require the critic persona to produce a table: risk, likelihood, impact, mitigation, owner.
Diagram creation: LLM generates C4 Mermaid from a pasted tree -L 2 repo structure. Never generate diagrams without a repo structure reference — invented components mislead.

example.code: ROLE: design-critic INPUT: docs/architecture/specs/spec.md and diagrams/*.mmd TASK: Risk-storm the design. Produce a table: | risk | likelihood | impact | mitigation | owner | Reject mitigations that are "add monitoring" without a concrete metric+threshold.

ROLE: tech-evaluator GOAL: Choose between <A>, <B>, <C> for <use case> with NFRs <...>. Output: weighted-score matrix with criteria, weights, scores, citations, and a recommendation that survives steel-manning the runner-up.

list.item: Pre-commit: naming conventions, file organization, import restrictions (ArchUnit / Packwerk / dependency-cruiser).
PR gate: if architecture-impacting paths change (infra/, deploy/, services/*/api/, docs/architecture/) without an ADR update → fail. See arch-review-check.sh in agent-integration.md.
Build: SonarQube (code smells, architectural anti-patterns), dependency analysis, API contract validation (OpenAPI diff).
Canary: automated smoke and load tests before full rollout. SLO parity required before advancing to next traffic percentage.

rule.statement: Treat each workflow as a pipeline of role-specialized subagents: clarifier, designer, critic, documenter. Materialize state to disk between steps (spec.md, options.md, adr-NNN.md) so agents reload from files, not transcript memory.
rule.rationale: Transcript memory is lost on context reset; disk state survives and allows parallel or sequential agent handoffs without information loss.

list.item: faion-brainstorm: diverge step in system-design and tech-evaluation workflows — generates multiple options with explicit trade-offs.
faion-sdd-execution: produces spec/design/test-plan triples and tracks lifecycle states against quality gates.
faion-feature-executor: drives migration plan execution task-by-task with checkpoint gates.
faion-improver: runs the after-action review 30 days post-decision against ADR consequences — checks if predictions held.
Review workflows degrade to rubber-stamping unless the critic agent has a separate persona and adversarial prompt with explicit failure-finding instructions.
Agents emit one giant "do everything" plan; force decomposition into the canonical phases and gate each step.
ATAM utility trees from LLMs are uniformly ranked; require explicit distribution (no more than 20% at highest tier).
Migration plans skip backfill, dual-write, and feature-flag cutover; inject those as required headings in the template.
ATAM/CBAM are heavyweight; teams under ~20 people rarely sustain them. Use lightweight risk-storming for small teams.
Human-in-loop gates are non-negotiable: ADR acceptance, evaluation outcome sign-off, migration go/no-go. Wire as approvals, not comments.
